verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 32–34, XVIII.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they heard of the resurrection of the dead, some mocked: and others said, We will hear thee again of this matter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So Paul departed from among them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Howbeit certain men clave unto him, and believed: among the which was Dionysius the Areopagite and a woman named Damaris, and others with them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: After these things Paul departed from Athens, and came to Corinth.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What can be the reason that, having persuaded (some so far as to say) that they would hear him again, and there being no dangers, Paul is so in haste to leave Athens?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Probably he knew that he should do them no great good; moreover he was led by the Spirit to Corinth. (b) For the Athenians, although fond of hearing strange things, nevertheless did not attend (to him); for this was not their study, but only to be always having something to say; which was the cause that made them hold off from him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if this was their custom, how is it that they accuse him, “he seemeth to be a setter forth of strange gods?” (ch. xvii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes, but these were matters they did not at all know what to make of.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Howbeit, he did convert both Dionysius the Areopagite, and some others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For those who were careful of (right) living, quickly received the word; but the others not so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It seemed to Paul sufficient to have cast the seeds of the doctrines. (a) To Corinth then, as I said, he was led by the Spirit, in which city he was to abide. (c) “And having found a certain Jew named Aquila, of Pontus by birth, lately come from Italy”—for the greater part of his life had been passed there—“and Priscilla his wife, because that Claudius had commanded all the Jews to depart from Rome.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For though it was in the reign of Nero that the war against the Jews was consummated, yet from the time of Claudius and thenceforward it was fanning up, at a distance indeed, so that, were it but so, they might come to their senses, and from Rome they were now driven as common pests.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is why it is so ordered by Providence that Paul was led thither as a prisoner, that he might not as a Jew be driven away, but as acting under military custody might even be guarded there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Having found these,) “he came to them, and because he was of the same craft, he abode with them and wrought: for by occupation they were tent-makers.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo, what a justification he found for dwelling in the same house with them!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For because here, of all places, it was necessary that he should not receive, as he himself says, “That wherein they glory, they may be found, even as we” (2 Cor. xi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 12), it is providentially ordered that he there abides.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he reasoned in the synagogue every sabbath, and persuaded the Jews and the Greeks.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when Silas and Timotheus were come from Macedonia, Paul was straitened in the word, testifying to the Jews that Jesus is the Christ.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when the Jews opposed and blasphemed,” i.e. they tried to bear him down (ἐπηρέαζον), they set upon him—What then does Paul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He separates from them, and in a very awful manner: and though he does not now say, “It was need that the word should be spoken unto you,” yet he darkly intimates it to them:—“and when they opposed themselves, and blasphemed, he shook his raiment, and said unto them, Your blood be upon your own heads; I am clean: from henceforth I will go unto the Gentiles.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he departed thence, and entered into a certain man’s house, named Justus, one that worshipped God, whose house joined hard to the synagogue.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how having again said, “Henceforth—” for all that, he does not neglect them; so that it was to rouse them that he said this, and thereupon came to Justus, whose house was contiguous to the synagogue, so that even from this they might have jealousy, from the very proximity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Crispus, the chief ruler of the synagogue, believed on the Lord with all his house.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This also was, of all things, enough to bring them over.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And many of the Corinthians hearing believed, and were baptized.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then spake the Lord to Paul in the night by a vision, Be not afraid, but speak, and hold not thy peace: for I am with thee, and no man shall set on thee to hurt thee: for I have much people in this city.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See by how many reasons He persuades him, and how He puts last the reason which of all others most prevailed with him, “I have much people in this city.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then how was it, you may ask, that they set upon him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet, the writer tells us, they prevailed nothing, but brought him to the proconsul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he continued there a year and six months, teaching the word of God among them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when Gallio was proconsul of Achaia, the Jews made insurrection with one accord against Paul, and brought him to the judgment-seat.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark why those men were ever contriving to give a public turn to the misdemeanors (they accused them of)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus see here: (b) “Saying, This fellow seduceth men contrary to the law to worship God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when Paul was about to open his mouth, Gallio said: If indeed it were any wrong-doing or wicked lewdness, O ye Jews, reason would that I should bear with you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if it be a question of words and names, and of your law, look ye to it; for I will be no judge of such matters.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he drave them from the judgment-seat.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This Gallio seems to me to have been a sensible man. (a) Thus observe, when these had said, “Against the law he seduceth men to worship God,” he “cared for none of these things:” and observe how he answers them: “If indeed it were” any matter affecting the city, “any wrong-doing or wicked lewdness,” etc. (c) “Then all the Jews took Sosthenes the ruler of the synagogue, and beat him before the judgment-seat: and Gallio cared for none of these things” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17): but their beating him he did not take as an insult to himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So petulant were the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us look over again what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they heard,” (ch. xvii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 32) what great and lofty doctrines, they did not even attend, but jeered at the Resurrection!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For the natural man,” it saith, “receiveth not the things of the Spirit.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And so,” it says, “Paul went forth.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Having persuaded some; derided by others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But certain men,” it says, “clave unto him, and believed, among whom was also Dionysius the Areopagite and some others.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And after these things,” etc. “And having found a certain Jew by name Aquila, of Pontus by birth, lately come from Italy, because that Claudius had ordered all Jews to depart from Rome, he came to them, and because he was of the same craft, he abode with them, and wrought: for by their occupation they were tent-makers.” (ch.xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Being of Pontus, this Aquila * * * .

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how, not in Jerusalem, nor near it (the crisis), was hasting to come, but at a greater distance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And with him he abides, and is not ashamed to abide, nay, for this very reason he does abide, as having a suitable lodging-place, for to him it was much more suitable than any king’s palace.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And smile not thou, beloved, to hear (of his occupation).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For (it was good for him) even as to the athlete the palæstra is more useful than delicate carpets; so to the warrior the iron sword (is useful), not that of gold.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And wrought,” though he preached.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us be ashamed, who though we have no preaching to occupy us, live in idleness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he disputed in the synagogue every sabbath day, and persuaded both Jews and Greeks” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 4): but “when they opposed and blasphemed” he withdrew, by this expecting to draw them more.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For wherefore having left that house did he come to live hard by the synagogue? was it not for this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it was not that he saw any danger here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But therefore it is that Paul having testified to them—not teaches now, but testifies—“having shaken his garments,” to terrify them not by word only but by action, “said unto them, Your blood be upon your own heads” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 6): he speaks the more vehemently as having already persuaded many.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I,” says he, “am clean.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then we also are accountable for the blood of those entrusted to us, if we neglect them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “From this time forth I will go to the Gentiles.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that also when he says, “Henceforth let no man trouble me” (Gal. vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17), he says it to terrify.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For not so much did the punishment terrify, as this stung them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And having removed thence he came into the house of one named Justus, that worshipped God, whose house was contiguous to the synagogue” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 7), and there abode, by this wishing to persuade them that he was in earnest (πρὸς τὰ ἐθνη ἠπείγετο) to go to the Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Accordingly, mark immediately the ruler of the synagogue converted, and many others, when he had done this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Crispus the ruler of the synagogue believed in the Lord, with his whole house: and many of the Corinthians hearing believed, and were baptized.”—(v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “With his whole house:” observe the converts in those times doing this with their entire household.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This Crispus he means where he writes, “I baptized none save Crispus and Gaius.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This (same) I take to be called Sosthenes—(evidently) a believer, insomuch that he is beaten, and is always present with Paul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the Lord said in the night,” etc. Now even the number (of the “much people”) persuaded him, but Christ’s claiming them for His own (moved him) more.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yet He says also, “Fear not:” for the danger was become greater now, both because more believed, and also the ruler of the synagogue.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This was enough to rouse him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not that he was reproved as fearing; but that he should not suffer aught; “I am with thee, and none shall set upon thee to hurt thee.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For He did not always permit them to suffer evil, that they might not become too weak.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For nothing so grieved Paul, as men’s unbelief and setting themselves (against the Truth): this was worse than the dangers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore it is that (Christ) appears to him now.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he continued a year and six months,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: After the year and six months, they set upon him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when Gallio was proconsul of Achaia,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 12, 13), because they had no longer the use of their own laws. (c) And observe how prudent he is: for he does not say straightway, I care not, but, “If,” says he, “it were a matter of wrong-doing or wicked lewdness, O ye Jews, reason would that I should bear with you; but if it be a question of doctrine and words and of your law, see ye to it, for I do not choose to be a judge of such matters.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 14, 15.) (g) He taught them that not such are the matters which crave a judicial sentence, but they do all things out of order.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, It is not my duty, but, “I do not choose,” that they may not trouble him again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus Pilate said in the case of Christ, “Take ye Him, and judge him according to your law.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (John xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they were just like men drunken and mad. (d) “And he drave them from the judgment-seat” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 16)—he effectually closed the tribunal against them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then all” (the Jews) “having seized Sosthenes the ruler of the synagogue, beat him before the judgment-seat.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Gallio cared for none of these things.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17). (a) This thing, of all others, set them on (to this violence)—their persuasion that the governor would not even let himself down (to notice it). (e) It was a splendid victory.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: O the shame they were put to! (b) For it is one thing to have come off victorious from a controversy, and another for those to learn that he cared nothing for the affair. (f) “And Gallio cared for none of these things:” and yet the whole was meant as an insult to him!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, forsooth, as if they had received authority (they did this).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why did he (Sosthenes), though he also had authority, not beat (them)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they were (otherwise) trained: so that the judge should learn which party was more reasonable.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This was no small benefit to those present—both the reasonableness of these, and the audacity of those. (h) He was beaten, and said nothing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This man let us also imitate: to them that beat us, let us return blow for blow, by meekness, by silence, by long-suffering.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: More grievous these wounds, greater this blow, and more heavy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For to show that it is not the receiving a blow in the body that is grievous, but the receiving it in the mind, we often smite people, but since it is in the way of friendship, they are even pleased: but if you smite any indifferent person in an insolent manner, you have pained him exceedingly, because you have touched his heart.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So let us smite their heart.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But that meekness inflicts a greater blow than fierceness, come, let us prove, so far as that is possible, by words.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the sure proof indeed is by acts and by experience: but if you will, let us also make the enquiry by word, though indeed we have often made it already.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now in insults, nothing pains us so much, as the opinion passed by the spectators; for it is not the same thing to be insulted in public and in private, but those same insults we endure even with ease, when we suffer them in a solitary place, and with none by to witness them, or know of them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So true is it that it is not the insult, as it is in itself, that mortifies us, but the having to suffer it in the sight of all men: since if one should do us honor in the sight of all men, and insult us in private, we shall notwithstanding even feel obliged to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The pain then is not in the nature of the insult, but in the opinion of the beholders; that one may not seem to be contemptible.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then, if this opinion should be in our favor?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is not the man attempting to disgrace us himself more disgraced, when men give their opinion in our favor?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, whom do the bystanders despise?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Him who insults, or him who being insulted keeps silence?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Passion indeed suggests, that they despise him who is insulted: but let us look into it now while we are free from that excitement, in order that we may not be carried away when the time comes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, whom do we all condemn?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Plainly the man who insults: and if he be an inferior, we shall say that he is even mad; if an equal, that he is foolish; if a superior, still we shall not approve of it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For which man, I ask, is worthy of approval, the man who is excited, who is tossed with a tempest of passion, who is infuriated like a wild beast, who demeans himself in this sort against our common nature, or he who lives in a state of calm, in a haven of repose, and in virtuous equanimity?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is not the one like an angel, the other not even like a man?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the one cannot even bear his own evils, while the other bears even those of others also: here, the man cannot even endure himself; there, he endures another too: the one is in danger of shipwreck, the other sails in safety, his ship wafted along the favoring gales: for he has not suffered the squall of passion to catch his sails and overturn the bark of his understanding: but the breath of a soft and sweet air fanning upon it, the breath of forbearance, wafts it with much tranquillity into the haven of wise equanimity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And like as when a ship is in danger of foundèring, the sailors know not what they cast away, whether what they lay hands upon be their own or other men’s property, but they throw overboard all the contents without discrimination, alike the precious and what is not such: but when the storm has ceased, then reckoning up all that they have thrown out, they shed tears, and are not sensible of the calm for the loss of what they have thrown overboard: so here, when passion blows hard, and the storm is raised, people in flinging out their words know not how to use order or fitness; but when the passion has ceased, then recalling to mind what kind of words they have given utterance to, they consider the loss and feel not the quiet, when they remember the words by which they have disgraced themselves, and sustained most grievous loss, not as to money, but as to character for moderation and gentleness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Anger is a darkness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The fool,” saith Scripture, “hath said in his heart, There is no God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Perhaps also of the angry man it is suitable to say the same, that the angry man hath said, There is no God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, saith Scripture, “Through the multitude of his anger he will not seek” (after God).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For let what pious thought will enter in, (passion) thrusts and drives all out, flings all athwart. (b) When you are told, that he whom you abused uttered not one bitter word, do you not for this feel more pain than you have inflicted? (a) If you in your own mind do not feel more pain than he whom you have abused, abuse still; (but) though there be none to call you to account, the judgment of your conscience, having taken you privately, shall give you a thousand lashes, (when you think) how you poured out a flood of railings on one so meek, and humble, and forbearing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We are forever saying these things, but we do not see them exhibited in works.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You, a human being, insult your fellow-man?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You, a servant, your fellow-servant?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But why do I wonder at this, when many even insult God?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let this be a consolation to you when suffering insult.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Are you insulted?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God also is insulted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Are you reviled?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God also was reviled.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Are you treated with scorn?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, so was our Master also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In these things He shares with us, but not so in the contrary things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For He never insulted another unjustly: God forbid!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He never reviled, never did a wrong.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that we are those who share with Him, not ye.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For to endure when insulted is God’s part: to be merely abusive, is the part of the devil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See the two sides.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Thou hast a devil” (John vii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 20; ib. xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22), Christ was told: He received a blow on the face from the servant of the high-priest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They who wrongfully insult, are in the same class with these.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if Peter was even called “Satan” (Matt. xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 23) for one word; much more shall these men, when they do the works of the Jews, be called, as those were called, “children of the devil” (John viii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 44), because they wrought the works of the devil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You insult; who are you, I ask (that you do so)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, rather the reason why you insult, is this, that you are nothing: no one that is human insults.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that what is said in quarrels, “Who are you?” ought to be put in the contrary way: “Insult: for you are nothing.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Instead of that the phrase is, “Who are you, that you insult?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “A better man than you,” is the answer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet it is just the contrary: but because we put the question amiss, therefore they answer amiss: so that the fault is ours.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as if we thought it was for great men to insult, therefore we ask, “Who are you, that you insult?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And therefore they make this answer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, on the contrary, we ought to say: “Do you insult? insult still: for you are nobody:” whereas to those who do not insult this should be said: “Who are you that you insult not?—you have surpassed human nature.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is nobility, this is generosity, to speak nothing ungenerous, though a man may deserve to have it spoken to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Tell me now, how many are there who are not worthy to be put to death?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nevertheless, the judge does not this in his own person, but interrogates them; and not this either, in his own person.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if it is not to be suffered, that the judge, sitting in judgment, should (in his own person) speak with a criminal, but he does all by the intervention of a third person, much more is it our duty not to insult our equals in rank; for all the advantage we shall get of them will be, not so much to have disgraced them, as to be made to learn that we have disgraced ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well then, in the case of the wicked, this is why we must not insult (even them); in the case of the good there is another reason also because they do not deserve it: and for a third, because it is not right to be abusive.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But as things are, see what comes of it; the person abused is a man, and the person abusing is a man, and the spectators men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then? must the beasts come between them and settle matters? for only this is left.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when both the wrong-doers and those who delight in the wrong-doing are men, the part of reconciler is left for the beasts: for just as when the masters quarrel in a house, there is nothing left but for the servants to reconcile them,—even if this be not the result, for the nature of the thing demands this,—just so is it here.—Are you abusive?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well may you be so, for you are not even human.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Insolence seemed to be a high-born thing; it seemed to belong to the great; whereas it belongs rather to slaves; but to give good words belongs to free men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as to do ill is the part of those, so to suffer ill is the part of these.—Just as if some slave should steal the master’s property, some old hag,—such a thing as that is the abusive man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And like as some detestable thief and runaway, with studied purpose stealing in, looks all around him, wishing to filch something: so does this man, even as he, look narrowly at all on every side, studying how to throw out some (reproach).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or perhaps we may set him forth by a different sort of example.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just as if one should steal filthy vessels out of a house, and bring them out in the presence of all men, the things purloined do not so disgrace the persons robbed, as they disgrace the thief himself: just so this man, by bringing out his words in the presence of all men, casts disgrace not on others but on himself by the words, in giving vent to this language, and be-fouling both his tongue and his mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is all one, when we quarrel with bad men, as if one for the sake of striking a man who is immersed in putrefying filth should defile himself by plunging his hands into the nastiness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore, reflecting on these things, let us flee the mischief thence accruing, and keep a clean tongue, that being clear from all abusiveness, we may be enabled with strictness to pass through the life present, and to attain unto the good things promised to those that love Him, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost together be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
